Totally. And this file-system routing is great. And how many
lines of code, how much work, or how many tokens would one have to burn and
increase the boilerplate and bundle size, and not do it in a way that
is standardized, like in Analog's meta-framework. If you do it from
scratch in Angular for this type of system you are writing, let's say a static
site, leveraging the benefits of SSG (Static Site Generation) to avoid
overloading the client with JavaScript that could have been resolved on the server side
beforehand, and promoting search engine visibility, this is a choice that
is a complete no-brainer.
Exactly. And building my blog helped me learn more about how it works under the hood,
because you get curious when you see that putting a file of a certain type with a certain syntax in
a directory just works. I wanted to know how that magic worked. It's a tremendously
complex codebase, and Brandon deserves huge credit because building something like that when maybe only
Copilot existed to suggest code is, as you say, a no-brainer. It's code that I consider art,
and it works wonderfully. We know it's a meta-framework, built on top of another framework.
So what it does is check your files depending on where you put them. There are conventions in the docs,
and based on those it creates the route exactly as Angular would. In the end, if you need manual
adjustments, or if you are not a fan of file-based routing, you can still use Angular's native routing
without giving up other things Analog provides, like Nitro support to deploy anywhere. We can talk
about that. Exactly. That was one of the easiest things to build something so simple. So I'd
say Analog is a simple choice for blogs, for content-rich sites. Why? Because it's much easier than
using Angular as a normal framework. And Angular has improved a lot recently; general API
changes made the learning curve much gentler, in my opinion. But the fact that it is a very
opinionated and rigid framework, though it decoupled from project dependencies in recent versions...
Many people collaborating on Analog are also part of the Angular community, so it's natural
that Analog's conventions helped greenlight certain features in Angular that weren't being
addressed, seeing the momentum Analog had.
For example, one of the things I saw almost immediately, which I think was inevitable, but it
helped the Angular team decide to implement their own Vite integration, because they saw a clear
demonstration that it worked. Obviously, I'm not involved in framework decisions, but I like to
think it raised questions in the community. I remember when Chau and Brandon created the .ng
files, which simplified the nomenclature thanks to the pioneering work they did.
So, several features implemented in the framework have helped. Since Analog builds on top of
Angular, if there's a new feature in Angular, it naturally works in Analog because it is built
on top. At the same time, we can experiment with concepts outside of Angular's rigidity.
A good example was in December when Brandon introduced Angular Skills in Analog, and only a month
and a half later, Mark Thompson, the DevRel Lead for Angular, introduced this same type of
skills to support developer knowledge and reduce token consumption. And Angular Skills became an
intrinsic part of the official Angular project. Exactly. So that's what I see in the project.
I feel there is inspiration that, due to internal processes at Google and the framework, can't
be accelerated as fast as the community wants. For example, selectorless components are highly
requested, and when Signals were introduced, people asked about Signal Forms. We will finally
see them stable in Angular 22. I love them; I'm ready to migrate our forms. Even my blog
has a small contact form that I migrated as soon as the stable API was released. And version
22 will stabilize features currently in preview, like Spartan, allowing headless components without rigid
HTML/CSS styles. Correct. In fact, one feature recently
implemented is internationalization. For my blog, I personally use Transloco, but then Brandon
implemented native internationalization in Angular which is inherited by Analog. It's on my to-do
list to migrate and see how the experience changes. I'll write about it since my goal was
to learn how it handles SEO, SSR, SSG, and content. I also wanted to contribute to the
community and get noticed. Sure. Staying up to date is not easy. I
think those who maintain libraries alongside a full-time job are like aliens. I have total
admiration for open source contributors. Absolutely. And Luis, backend in Angular was
historically external, but Analog integrates Nitro and H3 directly in the workflow. By allowing
API routes and middleware on the server, do you think this pushes the community towards
a unified full-stack? Hopefully. I think the more knowledge, the
better, for developers and engineers alike. What Analog brings with H3 and Nitro
and deploying almost anywhere by changing a few parameters is a no-brainer. At first,
Brandon suggested Netlify for my blog. I tried it, but didn't like the dashboard.
Having React projects on Vercel, I tried Vercel and it was amazing—changing one parameter was all
it took. Many frontend or backend developers struggle with infrastructure, so opening those doors
is huge. Server routes are simple: architecturally, keeping things close makes sense when they are in
the same domain. In a full-stack framework, it makes sense to have server routes where the page
lives. Nitro handles the server side, running a load function when the page is requested, fetching
data, creating HTML, and embedding it. Right, packaging it for deployment. Using H3
event handlers, Analog translates them to the format expected by each cloud provider. Whether you
deploy on Google Cloud Run, Firebase, AWS Lambda, Netlify, or Vercel, Nitro passively
handles adaptation to the runtime automatically. And I'm still learning about Nitro middlewares
because it's amazing how powerful it is. I'm exploring it since I want to suggest the framework
at work. To suggest a full-stack framework at an enterprise level, I prefer to be clear on
the backend, since I specialize more in frontend, but I'm curious by nature and look to learn. We
can always ask Brandon and other team members where this is headed.
Yes, when coordinating, you mentioned your enthusiasm for Vite 8 on the build pipeline,
placing Analog at the forefront of modern build tools. What do you think about project build
times and the benefits of the new Vite version? It looks amazing. Maybe many people don't know
that Angular's Vite implementation handles dev server differently than Analog. Analog does it for
dev and can leverage the plugin ecosystem. Exactly, all the plugins. That doesn't happen
with Angular due to technical limitations within Google's monorepo.
Exactly. Being a rigid framework, they might implement it in the future after refactoring.
Major frameworks like Vue, Solid, and others already use Vite, except for Next.js which does not.
We talked about what Analog brings. It's not the same as before because Angular SSR is now a
much better implemented package. This doesn't mean Analog doesn't add value; it can now inherit
features from Angular, like customer/server routes for hybrid rendering. This lets you define
if it will be pre-rendered, server-rendered with SSR, or client-rendered, which comes at a high
browser cost. Exactly. Not for our development machines with lots of RAM and minimal latency, but
thinking of slow connections or search engine bot crawlers. For those, client-side rendering
is not the best option. Exactly. My first contact with server-side
rendering was with Analog, and I didn't expect issues like flickers or parpades on the page. The
techniques I knew then are different from what I know now. These conventions existed but were
implemented with Angular bases, since Angular didn't give guidelines on how to do SSR/SSG.
Non-destructive incremental hydration prevents that white flash in the background that affects
Core Web Vitals and user experience. All three vitals were impacted, especially First Input Delay,
Interaction to Next Paint, and Cumulative Layout Shift.
Exactly. And returning to Vite, one of the things that boosted adoption was Vitest, which became
its own subproject in Analog and was implemented in Nx to add Vitest from scratch, and in Angular
as a plugin. Vite 8 improves this further with speed, as shown in Brandon's benchmarks. Analog and
Angular go hand in hand; innovation on one side opens doors on the other. This makes me excited
for where the framework is going. I still need to explore what doors Vite 8 opens for enterprise
environments, which are very different from personal blogs.
